path: 04 Resources/ETFA25 AAS Paper/ETFA25-000345 Bridging the Qualification Gap for the Asset Administration Shell - A Modular and Role-Based Learning Framework.pdf
text: Bridging the Qualification Gap for the Asset Administration Shell: A Modular and Role-Based Learning Framework Daniel Büttner , Dirk Schöttke , Stephan Schäfer HTW Berlin, Berlin, Germany dirk.schoettke@htw-berlin.de Sönke Knoch, Daniel Porta, Tim Schwartz DFKI, Saarbrücken, Germany soenke.knoch@dfki.de Claudette Ocando Röhricht XITASO GmbH, Leipzig, Germany claudette.ocando-roehricht@xitaso.com Aaron Zielstorff Fraunhofer IESE, Berlin, Germany aaron.zielstorff@iese.fraunhofer.de Andreas Bayha fortiss GmbH, München, Germany bayha@fortiss.org Abstract—The Asset Administration Shell (AAS) provides the digital foundation for interoperable systems in Industry 4.0. However, small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) face critical barriers in workforce qualification due to the lack of scalable training logic that aligns organizational maturity and functional roles with structured, rule-based learning paths. This paper presents a modular training framework that combines an empirically validated maturity model with role- specific learning paths. Learners are routed into one of three tracks—Initiate, Integrate, or Anchor—based on diagnostic input that reflects their organizational readiness and functional role profiles. The framework is implemented in a configurable Learning Management System (LMS) utilizing rule-based logic, including conditional access, content visibility controls, and progression triggers. Validation is grounded in a structured survey of German SMEs, which confirms heterogeneous maturity levels and training needs. The resulting system enables differentiated, scalable upskilling strategies aligned with AAS deployment, offering a practical basis for integration into industrial qualification programs and corporate training infrastructures. Index Terms—Asset Administration Shell (AAS), Adaptive Learning, Maturity Model, Hybrid Training I. I N T RO D U C T I O N A N D P RO B L E M S TAT E M E N T Although reference architectures such as RAMI 4.0 and IEC 63278 define the structural foundation for semantic interoperability, publications by Plattform Industrie 4.0 and DIN–DKE primarily describe theoretical structures without providing solid evidence of actual implementation of the AAS in practice—particularly not in SMEs [1], [2], [3], [4]. This limitation is less a matter of technical infrastructure than of qualification. Scalable, role-specific training architectures are still missing. Existing tools, such as the AASX Package Explorer [5], provide access to core functionalities; however, current qualification formats rarely consider operational roles or organizational maturity levels [6]. As a result, workforce development often fails to meet the practical requirements of AAS implementation in heterogeneous industrial settings. To address this gap, we propose a modular training frame- work that combines an empirically derived maturity model with rule-based learning path assignment. Based on diagnostic input, learners are routed into one of three training tracks—Initiate, Integrate, or Anchor—aligned to functional responsibilities and digital readiness. The architecture is implemented in a configurable open- source LMS, using role-based access control, conditional progression, and diagnostic-based routing. The framework logic reflects practical qualification needs and has been validated through a structured survey among German SMEs. It enables scalable, differentiated upskilling tailored to the industrial deployment of the AAS. II. T H E O R E T I C A L F O U N DAT I O N A N D R E L AT E D W O R K The AAS provides a model for cross-system exchange in Industry 4.0 infrastructures. As a digital representation of industrial assets, it facilitates standardized data structures and lifecycle-wide integration across distributed systems [7]. Its rel- evance as an interoperability-enabling interface has been widely acknowledged. However, the operational realization—especially in SMEs—remains limited due to high integration complexity, security concerns, and a lack of standardization for real-world applications [8]; one contributing factor is that many SMEs lack modular and targeted training structures [9]. While technical standards such as the AAS provide a consistent semantic foundation, they do not automatically translate into operational integration. Empirical educational research emphasizes that successful implementation requires instructional scaffolding tailored to varying competence levels and roles [10]. In many SMEs, however, training initiatives lack a modular structure or defined progression logic, which limits their ability to bridge the gap between specification and workforce adoption. While there are no dedicated diagnostic frameworks for AAS maturity, established models such as the Industry 4.0 Maturity Index and the Capability Maturity Model Integration (CMMI) have been applied to assess digital readiness and offer orientation for AAS-related development. These models provide structured assessments but do not directly translate into operational training architectures [11]. Their adaptation requires rule-based path assignment, content modularization, and role-specific access logic. Digital learning platforms such as Moodle offer suitable features to implement this logic, including role-based visibility and conditional unlocking [12]. These mechanisms support dif- ferentiated onboarding and structured competence development in heterogeneous user groups. III. D I DAC T I C C O N C E P T A N D L E A R N I N G A R C H I T E C T U R E A. From Training Needs to Learning Architecture The introduction of the AAS in industrial environments poses not only technical but also instructional challenges. Different levels of prior knowledge, heterogeneous role profiles, and different stages of organizational digital maturity necessitate a structured qualification strategy. To meet these demands, the BaSys4Transfer project designed a modular training architecture. Its instructional design is based on the ADDIE model [13], which guides the analysis of learner needs, the development of modular training components, and the deployment of tailored sequences within a digital learning environment. By merging structured instructional design with diagnostic instruments and rule-based content allocation, the training framework enables scalable delivery of AAS-related compe- tencies. It supports differentiation based on user role and maturity level, making it relevant to a wide range of industrial contexts. This approach contrasts with existing frameworks that concentrate on technical asset modeling or condition monitoring infrastructures [14]. B. Maturity Model as Control Logic The maturity model forms the diagnostic and control layer of the training architecture. It evaluates digital readiness across six levels (0–5) using five dimensions: technology usage, data practices, process orientation, strategic alignment, and employee involvement. A self-assessment enables organizations to position them- selves within this model. It relies on a structured survey instrument developed during the project, providing a reusable basis for future applications of the framework. Founded on the results, learners are routed into one of three learning paths: • Initiate – foundational AAS understanding, • Integrate – operational implementation and process inte- gration, • Anchor – strategic embedding and long-term scaling. Rule-Based Assignment Examples: Adaptive learning paths are assigned using rule-based logic derived from the organi- zation’s maturity level and the learner’s functional role. The following examples illustrate typical routing conditions: • Rule 1: IF Role = Technician AND Maturity Level = 1 THEN Assign Module = AAS-Basics • Rule 2: IF Role = IT Coordinator AND Maturity Level = 2 THEN Assign Module = AAS-Integration • Rule 3: IF Role = Manager AND Maturity Level <= 2 THEN Assign Module = AAS-StrategicOverview These rules are implemented within the LMS using condi- tional access settings, visibility filters, and activity completion triggers. Ini!ate Learning path types Integrate Anchor Strategic Integra!on and Process responsibility Opera!onal U!liza!on and Data integra!on Fundamentals and Awareness Fig. 1. Types of learning paths. Each path is composed of modular training units. The routing mechanism aligns path assignment with role-specific requirements—for example, shop floor operators may enter at the Initiate level, system integrators at Integrate, and strategy leads at Anchor. Figure 1 illustrates how these routes map to organizational readiness stages. C. Platform Design and Instructional Principles The training architecture is implemented using an extended Moodle platform. Modular content delivery is combined with scenario-based navigation, formative assessment, and function- specific routing. Modules span 10–30 minutes and employ diverse media formats including videos, quizzes, and webinars. Role-based cohort assignment, activity completion tracking, and access restriction mechanisms enable dynamic routing and progressive path unlocking on the platform. These rule- based mechanisms utilize Moodle’s built-in features, includ- ing conditional activity settings, role-based permissions, and progress-based visibility controls. Instructional sequencing follows four core principles: (1) scaffolded learning with gradual support reduction [10], [15], (2) conditional content access based on maturity and function [16], (3) workplace-oriented transfer [17], and (4) feedback-driven adaptation via learning analytics [18]. The architecture is aligned with constructive alignment [19] and instructional systems design [20], ensuring that outcomes, methods, and assessments form a coherent, scalable structure. Learner progression is shown in radar charts across the five maturity dimensions, providing a structured comparison of pre- and post-training effects without requiring detailed content diagnostics (see Figure 2). D. Scalability The modular learning architecture allows scalable deploy- ment across organizational settings and technical infrastructures. before a!er Use of Technology Data Management Process Orienta"on Strategic Integra"on Employees & Learning Culture Maturity Model Fig. 2. Maturity model visualizing development across five dimensions pre- and post-training. New content modules can be combined without disrupting exist- ing paths, allowing domain-specific extensions and incremental curriculum growth. Routing logic is determined via metadata structures, enabling dynamic mapping to evolving role profiles and maturity levels. Parallel onboarding of new user groups is supported through rule-based path allocation. Metadata fields are organized within the LMS using custom profile fields and tag-based activity labeling. This allows for automated rule interpretation and control over content display. Conditional access settings and activity restrictions in the LMS execute rule evaluation, enabling metadata-driven path logic without custom code. Interoperability is achieved through the use of standardized interfaces such as SCORM and LTI. Integration with external systems for single sign-on (SSO) and user provisioning allows seamless embedding into corporate IT environments. Granularity, sequencing, and access rights are fully config- urable to reflect sector-specific workflows and qualification objectives. IV. A DA P T I V E L E A R N I N G A R C H I T E C T U R E F O R AAS C O M P E T E N C E D E V E L O P M E N T A. Objective and Initial Situation The implementation of the AAS demands structured up- skilling across diverse functional roles. Training designs that overlook organizational maturity, digital infrastructure, and role- specific competence levels often fail to achieve effectiveness in SME contexts. A structured differentiation between roles—such as IT integrators versus production planners—and correspond- ing entry points is essential for scalable qualification architec- tures. Without a differentiated approach, training programs risk being either too abstract or too advanced for their intended audience. This diversity necessitates flexible training structures that adapt content and pace according to learners’ needs. To address this requirement, the proposed framework introduces a rule- based control logic tailored to role-specific requirements and maturity conditions. B. Learning Path Differentiation and Control Dimensions Learners are assigned to one of three modular paths—Initiate, Integrate, or Anchor—using a deterministic routing model. This model is structured along four control dimensions: 1) Functional role (e.g., IT, engineering, production plan- ning, management), 2) Digital maturity level, derived from organizational diagnostics, 3) Prior knowledge, identified via self-assessment or entry tests, 4) Learning objectives, such as recall, application, or decision-making [21]. Each learner begins with a shared orientation module before proceeding to their assigned path. The Initiate path focuses on awareness and foundational concepts, Integrate supports operational use cases, and Anchor addresses strategic adoption and system-level integration. The control logic is implemented via conditional access and progression rules within the learning management system. This enables transparent routing, avoids content overload, and aligns instruction with organizational needs and user capabilities. C. Empirical Foundation via Maturity Diagnosis To validate the adaptive training architecture and assess AAS- related readiness in SMEs, a maturity diagnosis was conducted among German manufacturing companies in Q2 2025. The survey examined five dimensions: technology use, data prac- tices, business processes, strategic alignment, and workforce development. Survey Design and Sample: The instrument consisted of 34 structured items across five domains. Distributed via industrial networks, the survey yielded 32 fully completed responses. Participants represented firms from 1 to 1,000+ employees across six federal states. Maturity Model and Scoring Logic: Each firm was evaluated using a six-level model (0–5) based on the BaSys4Transfer framework (see Table I). Level 0 denoted complete unawareness of AAS, while Level 5 indicated continuous optimization and integration. TABLE I AAS M AT U R I T Y L E V E L S A N D I N T E R P R E TAT I O N Level Description 0 – Unaware No awareness of AAS 1 – Initial Isolated digitalization, no AAS intent 2 – Awareness Fragmented initiatives, general interest 3 – Developing Partial integration, planned projects 4 – Managed Structured implementation incl. strategy 5 – Optimized Continuous improvement, full integration Scoring logic included rule-based mappings; for example, reporting ≥ 3 digital systems (e.g., ERP, MES, IoT) resulted in a technology score ≥ 3. Strategic maturity required documentation of AAS planning; workforce indicators included formal training programs. Key Findings: Only 50% of respondents were familiar with AAS; 43.8% were at Level 0 (Table II). The 50% familiarity rate refers to self-reported awareness levels as indicated in the survey responses. ERP systems were used by 87.5%, but only 25% had AAS-related training plans. Data maturity was inconsistent: 87.5% used automated analysis, yet 0% had designated AAS responsibilities. Additionally, 37.5% failed to meet any of the six data quality criteria. TABLE II D I S T R I B U T I O N O F C O M PA N I E S B Y AAS M AT U R I T Y L E V E L (n = 32) Maturity Level Share of Companies 0 – Unaware 43.8% 1 – Initial 12.5% 2 – Awareness 31.2% 3 – Developing 12.5% 4 – Managed 0.0% 5 – Optimized 0.0% Interpretation: The results indicate a highly fragmented maturity landscape across SMEs, characterized by significant variation in digital infrastructure, process integration, and strategic alignment regarding AAS adoption. Despite the widespread availability of digital infrastructure, the strategic integration of AAS remains low. The absence of Level 4 and 5 firms highlights a clear qualification gap. These findings underscore the importance of structured, differentiated training paths that are tailored to organizational readiness and learner profiles. V. T H E AAS C O M P E T E N C E F R A M E W O R K A. Objective and Positioning As industrial digitalization advances, the systematic qualifi- cation of technical and managerial staff becomes a key factor in workforce transformation. The AAS enables standardized communication across digitally networked production systems. However, effective implementation demands more than techni- cal knowledge—it requires a structured competence framework embedded in operational practice. The AAS Competence Framework addresses this need. It integrates core components—such as a maturity model, differen- tiated learning paths, and an adaptive learning architecture—into a coherent reference structure. It serves both as a design tool for role-specific training and as a diagnostic model for competence tracking. Unlike traditional curricula, which are often linear and role- agnostic, the framework employs a modular, maturity-based ap- proach aligned with existing European standards (e.g., EQF [22], DigComp 2.2 [23], and ECVET [24]). For instance, the modules reflect EQF level descriptors for autonomy and responsibility, and integrate competence areas from DigComp 2.2 such as digital content creation and problem solving. It is extensible to adjacent topics in industrial digitalization. Examples include the implementation of digital twins in logistics, predictive maintenance strategies, and low-code automation frameworks. The framework links individual upskilling to organizational transformation processes and supports scalable, technology- enabled learning strategies. The three learning tracks—Initiate, Integrate, and An- chor—reflect progressive levels of AAS competence devel- opment. Initiate fosters foundational understanding, Integrate supports applied implementation, and Anchor enables strategic adoption and cross-functional scaling. B. Architecture and Modularization The framework integrates control logic and content pro- gression across multiple layers. At its core lies a modular structure addressing role diversity, application contexts, and organizational maturity. Four core modules represent key domains of AAS implementation. The structure and learning objectives of the four core modules are summarized in Table III. Trainer roles are primarily addressed from Module 2 onward, as foundational technical knowledge is established in earlier stages. Each module consists of essential and optional units, which are dynamically activated based on role and maturity diagnostics. The modular structure supports flexible learning paths without requiring a fixed sequence. This enables depth where needed while preserving clarity and media continuity. C. Routing and Feedback Mechanisms The framework assigns learners to modular content based on three input parameters: • Role profile (e.g., technician, IT, manager), • Maturity level (AAS readiness: levels 0–5), • Individual profile (e.g., prior knowledge, goals, time constraints). These parameters are processed via rule-based logic to generate adaptive learning paths. A typical example is: IF Role = Engineer AND Level = 1 THEN Assign: Track = Integrate, Module = Application and Integration This logic is implemented in the LMS using deterministic mechanisms such as conditional visibility, scoring tables, and completion-based progression. Entry points are flexible: learners can start at intermediate levels if prior knowledge is demonstrated, and transition modules are available for changing roles. The adaptive routing logic is visualized in Figure 3. To ensure targeted progression, the framework integrates feedback mechanisms into four iterative stages: 1) Initial Diagnosis: Self-assessments and maturity diag- nostics are used to determine the starting point and assign modules. 2) Formative Feedback: In-module data (e.g., task com- pletion, quiz performance, time-on-task) is used to refine learning activities. Learners receive tailored feedback; instructors access aggregated dashboards. 3) Validation and Completion: Competence is demon- strated through application tasks and peer/self-evaluation. Learning artifacts contribute to digital portfolios and certification (e.g., open badges). 4) Looped Adaptation: Learning paths are adjusted based on performance and behavioral data. These feedback TABLE III OV E RV I E W O F C O R E M O D U L E S Module Target Group Competence Objective Contents 0: Orientation & Awareness All roles Understand AAS relevance in digital strategies Digital twin concepts, interoperability, RAMI 4.0, I4.0 stan- dards 1: Application & Inte- gration Technical staff, IT, co- ordinators Apply and integrate AAS elements Submodel configuration, AASX server usage, testbed integra- tion 2: Enablement & Transfer Trainers, team leads Ensure transfer and long- term usability Process redesign, internal communication, change manage- ment 3: Strategy & Innova- tion Executives, R&D, in- novation units Design and evaluate AAS strategies Standardization, data ethics, innovation, scalability Learner Role Proﬁle (e.g. Technician, Manager) Organiza"onal Maturity (5-stage AAS model) Individual Learning Proﬁl (prior knowledge, goals) Rule-Based Control Logic (decision trees, scoring, AI-ready) Assigned Learning Track Ini"ate / Integrade / Anchor Individualized Module Composi"on Module 0 - 3 + Op"onal Units Feedback & Analy"cs (progress, tutor input) Dynamic Adjustment Fig. 3. Adaptive learning path logic based on role, maturity level, and learner input. loops support system-wide refinement and instructional optimization. This integrated mechanism ensures role- and maturity-aligned learning progression and supports continuous improvement through data-driven insights. D. Technological Infrastructure and Implementation The qualification framework is deployed using an extended Moodle-based LMS with modular control logic, open interfaces, and tailored extensions. Key functionalities include: • Conditional content access based on role, maturity level, or assessment results, • Role-specific navigation and visibility settings, • Competency mapping aligned with learning activities, • Tracking, progress-based unlocking, and learner dash- boards. Learning content is delivered modularly using microlearn- ing units, simulations, application tasks, and reflection tools. Diagnostic data is processed through internal rule engines to adapt learning paths dynamically. To ensure scalability and interoperability, the infrastructure supports: • LTI interfaces for integration of external tools (e.g., simulations, feedback systems), • Open standards such as SCORM and xAPI, • Certification interfaces via industry partners, chambers, and academic institutions. All components are compatible with industrial IT environ- ments and comply with relevant data protection standards, including the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). This enables high scalability while maintaining both pedagogical integrity and technical robustness. E. Objective and Context To demonstrate the role- and maturity-based alignment of the proposed learning framework, this section introduces a hybrid training demonstrator developed within the AIQUAMA project, coordinated by the German Research Center for Artificial Intelligence (DFKI). The system aims to support competence development in AAS-related workflows by combining sensor integration, semantic data models, and intuitive human-machine interfaces. From a framework perspective, the demonstrator maps to the Integrate learning path and is intended for maintenance engineers in maturity levels 1–2. It is linked to Module 1 of the training architecture and supports learners in acquiring basic competencies in digital twin navigation and structured AAS interaction. The scenario builds on real-world production tasks, enabling practical engagement with key elements of AAS-based systems. F. Technical Setup and Interaction Flow The demonstrator utilizes the BaSyx software stack to model digital representations of car body components and corresponding worker instructions as structured submodels within the AAS. A humanoid robot (Pepper) serves as a natural language interface, equipped with speech synthesis and speech recognition capabilities that enable dialogue-based interaction with the environment. User queries are interpreted by a large language model (LLM), which accesses a Neo4j-based knowledge graph linked to the AAS ecosystem. This setup supports contextual queries about domain-specific parameters (e.g., door alignment) by providing visual feedback through augmented reality displays or physical projections. The setup exemplifies a shift from conventional digital twins toward what has been described as "Twinned AI"—a paradigm in which intelligent systems not only simulate but also autonomously optimize and interact with physical manufacturing environments. This conceptual advancement underscores the potential of integrating semantic AAS structures with agentic AI components to support immersive, hybrid learning experiences. DIGITAL TWIN AGENTIC LLM SYSTEM CAR BODY NACHHER Show the clearance on the car body! Now, you see the tile view projected on the car body. Fig. 4. AAS-based demonstrator for interactive clearance adjustment (Hannover Fair 2025). As illustrated in Figure 4, the demonstrator was showcased at Hannover Fair 2025, where it enabled real-time human- machine dialogue on clearance adjustments. The interaction was facilitated through multimodal interfaces, including the hu- manoid robot, visual projection, and augmented reality glasses. This configuration illustrates how intuitive, embodied access to AAS-based systems can enhance learner engagement, foster conceptual understanding, and promote transparency in training contexts. G. Didactic Integration and Learning Benefits The demonstrator is integrated into the Integrate learning path, particularly within Module 1, where participants engage with the practical application and operational principles of the AAS. By enabling real-time interaction with digital twin functionalities, the immersive environment facilitates experi- ential learning and strengthens conceptual understanding. The multimodal setup—combining physical components, augmented feedback, and dialogue-based interfaces—supports the explo- ration of key AAS constructs such as submodels, data access mechanisms, and lifecycle orchestration. Learners are further encouraged to critically reflect on human-AAS interaction, interface design, and the transferability of use cases to their own industrial contexts. Initial feedback from the demonstrator’s deployment at Hannover Fair 2025 highlighted the potential of natural lan- guage interfaces to lower entry barriers and stimulate learner engagement. These findings support the use of multimodal, embodied systems in the Integrate learning path and reinforce the practical relevance of AAS-based training environments. H. Scenario-Based Exploration of Framework Scalability To assess the applicability of the proposed learning archi- tecture beyond the initial demonstrator, a set of illustrative scenarios was constructed. These examples do not reflect validated field implementations but were derived from recurring patterns in AAS adoption and typical qualification challenges across different industrial domains. They serve to map the role- specific learning paths—Initiate, Integrate, and Anchor—onto plausible operational contexts characterized by varying digital maturity levels and stakeholder roles. 1) Use Case: Manual Switch Cabinet Assembly with Digital Product Passport: Challenge. In SMEs with predominantly manual production processes, traceability and documentation quality remain critical issues. The introduction of a digital product passport, implemented via AAS, aims to improve trans- parency, process reliability, and compliance without disrupting established workflows. Involved Roles and Learning Paths. Production Manager (Anchor path): Responsible for initi- ating the digital product passport strategy and integrating it into manual assembly lines. Develops standard procedures for documenting manual steps within the AAS submodel structure. IT Engineer (Integrate path): Implements mobile input sys- tems (e.g., tablets) for real-time process documentation. Ensures that data entries are correctly linked to the corresponding AAS elements and accessible throughout the lifecycle. Production Operator (Initiate path): Utilizes handheld de- vices to input task-specific data during assembly. Receives basic training on AAS-related input mechanisms and data relevance. Learning Objectives. - Enable structured documentation of manual steps using AAS-compliant metadata. - Familiarize all roles with the digital product passport as a cross-functional data layer. - Build competence in applying AAS elements to enhance shopfloor transparency. Results. The use case resulted in traceable assembly records that are linked directly to product variants and production orders. Operators documented their work with minimal over- head. IT staff ensured integration into existing MES systems, while production managers leveraged AAS data for workflow improvements and quality tracking across batches. 2) Use Case: Predictive Maintenance in Machinery Manu- facturing: Challenge. To increase asset efficiency and reduce maintenance costs, a machinery manufacturer introduces predic- tive maintenance strategies based on real-time operational data. The integration of AAS structures aims to enhance coordination among sensor systems, shopfloor operations, and management- level decision support. Involved Roles and Learning Paths. IT Engineer (Integrate path): Responsible for deploying IoT sensor networks across critical machines and developing middleware components to aggregate condition monitoring data. Connects sensor streams to AAS submodels and integrates output with ERP and MES systems for centralized analysis and visualization. Production Engineer (Initiate path): Utilizes AAS-based dashboards and analytics to assess machine health, detect anomalies, and schedule targeted interventions. Applies op- erational insights to optimize maintenance routines and reduce downtime. TABLE IV M A P P I N G O F U S E C A S E S T O RO L E S, L E A R N I N G PAT H S, M AT U R I T Y L E V E L S, A N D O B J E C T I V E S Use Case Role Learning Path Maturity Level Core Learning Objective Switch Cabinet Assembly with Digital Product Passport Production Manager / IT Engi- neer / Operator Anchor / Integrate / Initiate 2–3 Enable AAS-based process doc- umentation and improve trans- parency in manual workflows Predictive Maintenance in Ma- chinery Manufacturing IT Engineer / Production Engi- neer Integrate / Initiate 2–3 Apply AAS for condition mon- itoring and maintenance opti- mization Compliance and Traceability in Automotive Supply Chains Executive / IT Expert Anchor / Integrate 3–4 Use AAS for compliance, de- centralized documentation, and risk reduction Learning Objectives. - Build technical understanding of condition monitoring using AAS interfaces. - Learn to navigate AAS submodels for diagnostic and prognostic purposes. - Apply AAS-integrated data to improve maintenance workflows and asset lifecycle planning. Results. The company reported a reduction in unplanned downtime within the first operational year. Production engineers adopted AAS tools for planning and responsiveness, while IT staff ensured reliable integration and data quality. The use case confirmed the effectiveness of role-specific training in modular diagnostics and system orchestration. 3) Use Case: Compliance and Traceability in Automotive Supply Chains: Challenge. To meet increasing compliance demands and reduce the risk of product recalls, an automotive supplier integrates AAS-based tracking mechanisms across its component supply chain. The system enables end-to-end traceability and verifiability of part-level information. Involved Roles and Learning Paths. Executive Management (Anchor path): Defines the digital- ization roadmap and mandates the use of AAS as a standard mechanism for component documentation. Aligns internal processes and supplier requirements with audit and regulatory expectations. IT Expert (Integrate path): Develops a blockchain-enhanced infrastructure to ensure tamper-proof documentation and data lineage. Connects AAS instances to the distributed ledger to validate component origin, status, and compliance metadata. Learning Objectives. - Understand the application of AAS to compliance and supply chain transparency. - Learn to imple- ment secure, decentralized data protocols via AAS–blockchain integration. - Utilize AAS metadata for quality assurance and recall risk mitigation. Results. The introduction of the traceability system led to a decrease in product recalls over the following year. Management gained real-time insight into part movements and compliance status. IT departments achieved significant gains in data trustworthiness and response times in QA-critical scenarios. To enhance comparability, Table IV provides a consolidated overview of the use cases, including the involved roles, learning paths, maturity levels, and core learning objectives. VI. C O N C L U S I O N A N D F U T U R E W O R K The proposed AAS Competence Framework provides a modular and maturity-aligned structure for role-specific up- skilling in industrial digitalization. Designed for workplace integration, the architecture backs personalized learning paths across engineering, IT, and operations roles, while maintaining compatibility with European qualification frameworks, includ- ing EQF, DigComp, and IEC 63278. The system applies to company training, reskilling, and certification programs, and can be extended to related domains such as OPC UA or edge computing. By combining technical rule logic with pedagogical diagnostics, the framework enables adaptive control over competence progression and feedback- based learning. Initial deployments indicate preliminary usability and role alignment, particularly in SME settings, as observed during internal piloting activities (Section IV-C). Long-term adoption relies on stable IT environments, structured learning content, and organizational support. Future efforts will focus on vali- dating scenario-based mappings, piloting certification-oriented modules, and integrating diagnostic feedback loops to enable a scalable rollout. From a research perspective, further studies are needed to investigate long-term competence development, real-time learner adaptation, and ethical aspects such as algorithmic transparency and content ownership across AAS-based learning processes. Additionally, the illustrative use cases presented in this paper require empirical validation to assess their practical relevance, transferability, and role-specific learning impact in operational training environments (Section V-H). VII. AC K N OW L E D G E M E N T This work was supported by the German Federal Ministry of Research, Technology and Space under grant 01IS22089G as part of the “BaSys4Transfer” project. Special thanks go to Volkswagen—Kai Warsönke and Henrik Waschke—for providing the use case developed with DFKI within the AIQUAMA (01IS22032) project, funded by BMFTR. The authors thank all project partners for their valuable contributions.
